meta.title: DBX-DAA-1: SQL Foundations and Lakehouse Queries

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to run interactive SQL queries against a large Delta table in Databricks SQL. Which compute resource should they attach their queries to?

options.text: A SQL Warehouse (formerly SQL Endpoint)
options.explanation: SQL Warehouses are the compute resource designed specifically for running SQL queries in Databricks SQL, providing optimized performance for interactive analytics.

options.text: An all-purpose cluster configured in the Compute section
options.explanation: All-purpose clusters are used in notebooks and are not available in the Databricks SQL interface.

options.text: A job cluster defined in a Databricks Workflow
options.explanation: Job clusters are ephemeral resources for automated workflows, not interactive queries.

options.text: A single-node cluster with the Photon runtime
options.explanation: Single-node clusters are notebook-based compute and are not selectable within Databricks SQL.

questions.stem: A data analyst notices that identical queries submitted by different team members return results at different speeds. Some queries finish in under a second while others take 30 seconds. The queries are running on the same SQL Warehouse. Which feature is most likely responsible for the fast responses?

options.text: Photon engine parallelizing the query execution
options.explanation: Photon accelerates execution but does not explain sub-second results for complex queries — it still needs to scan data.

options.text: Auto-scaling adding additional clusters to handle concurrent users
options.explanation: Auto-scaling reduces concurrency wait time but does not make a query itself faster.

options.text: The disk cache (I/O cache) warming up remote storage files
options.explanation: The disk cache warms up file reads but does not skip execution entirely.

options.text: The query result cache returning cached results for repeated identical queries
options.explanation: The query result cache stores the exact output of a query for up to 24 hours and returns it instantly when the same query is resubmitted against data that has not changed, regardless of which user submits it.

questions.stem: A business analyst submits a query and receives results in 2 seconds. A colleague submits the exact same query 10 minutes later against the same warehouse, but the underlying Delta table was updated with new rows 5 minutes ago. What result will the colleague receive?

options.text: The cached result from the original query, since the cache lasts 24 hours
options.explanation: Table modifications invalidate the cache regardless of how much time has passed.

options.text: An error indicating the query cache is stale and must be manually cleared
options.explanation: No error is raised — the system transparently re-executes the query.

options.text: Fresh results including the new rows, because the table change invalidates the result cache
options.explanation: The Databricks SQL query result cache is automatically invalidated when the underlying table data changes. After the Delta table is updated, any subsequent query will execute fresh against the new data.

options.text: The cached result, but only if the new rows represent less than 10% of the table
options.explanation: There is no percentage-based threshold for cache invalidation; any change to the table invalidates cached results.

questions.stem: An analyst needs to review all queries run by a specific team member over the past week to troubleshoot a performance issue. Where in Databricks SQL should the analyst look?

options.text: The Query Editor history tab, filtered by user and date range
options.explanation: The Query Editor history is limited to queries run in that specific editor session by the current user.

options.text: The SQL Warehouse event log in the Compute section
options.explanation: The warehouse event log tracks cluster start/stop events, not individual query executions.

options.text: The Databricks workspace audit log accessible only to administrators
options.explanation: Query History is accessible to users with appropriate permissions and does not require workspace-level administrator access.

options.text: The Query History page in Databricks SQL, filtered by user and time
options.explanation: The Query History page in Databricks SQL allows analysts and administrators to browse all previously executed queries with filters for user, warehouse, status, date range, and duration.

questions.stem: A SQL Warehouse is configured as Serverless with a cluster size of Medium (X-Large) and auto-stop set to 10 minutes. An analyst submits a query at 9:00 AM, another at 9:05 AM, and a third at 9:25 AM. Which queries will experience a cold-start delay?

options.text: Only the first query at 9:00 AM
options.explanation: The third query at 9:25 AM also experiences a cold start due to the auto-stop.

options.text: The first query at 9:00 AM and the third query at 9:25 AM
options.explanation: The warehouse starts cold at 9:00 AM (first query triggers a start), remains active through 9:05 AM (second query runs warm), but auto-stops after 10 minutes of idle time — by 9:25 AM it has been idle since ~9:05 AM, exceeding the 10-minute auto-stop threshold, so it must restart.

options.text: All three queries, because serverless warehouses always cold-start
options.explanation: Serverless warehouses are generally faster to start than classic warehouses, but they do still have a startup period after auto-stop.

options.text: Only the third query at 9:25 AM, because serverless has no cold-start
options.explanation: While serverless start times are faster, they still incur a startup delay after the warehouse has stopped.

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to receive an email notification when a daily sales metric drops below $10,000. The metric is calculated by a saved SQL query. Which Databricks SQL feature should the analyst use?

options.text: A dashboard refresh schedule set to run daily
options.explanation: A dashboard refresh schedule updates the visualization but does not send notifications based on data conditions.

options.text: A parameterized query with a HAVING clause that filters results below $10,000
options.explanation: A HAVING clause filters query results but does not generate notifications.

options.text: A Databricks SQL Alert configured with a threshold condition on the query result
options.explanation: Databricks SQL Alerts allow you to set threshold-based conditions on a saved query result and trigger notifications via configured destinations (email, Slack, PagerDuty, webhooks) when the condition is met.

options.text: A Databricks Workflow job that emails when a notebook cell output is below the threshold
options.explanation: While Databricks Workflows can notify on job completion, configuring notebook-based email alerts is more complex and is not the purpose-built Databricks SQL feature.

questions.stem: A team of analysts shares a SQL Warehouse. An analyst has the CAN_USE permission on the warehouse. A manager wants to ensure that only the manager can change the warehouse configuration (cluster size, auto-stop settings). Which permission should be granted to the manager but NOT the analysts?

options.text: CAN_RUN
options.explanation: CAN_RUN is not a warehouse-level permission — it applies to queries and dashboards.

options.text: CAN_EDIT
options.explanation: CAN_EDIT is not a warehouse permission level.

options.text: CAN_MANAGE
options.explanation: CAN_MANAGE on a SQL Warehouse grants the ability to modify warehouse configuration including cluster size, auto-stop, and scaling settings, as well as manage permissions on the warehouse itself.

options.text: IS_OWNER
options.explanation: IS_OWNER is not a separate permission that can be granted — ownership is set at creation time and transferred separately.

questions.stem:
A data analyst runs the following command:

```sql
DESCRIBE DETAIL sales.monthly_revenue;
```

What information will this command return?

options.text: The full history of all operations performed on the monthly_revenue table
options.explanation: The full operation history is returned by DESCRIBE HISTORY, not DESCRIBE DETAIL.

options.text: The row count and null statistics for each column in the monthly_revenue table
options.explanation: Row counts and null statistics are returned by ANALYZE TABLE, not DESCRIBE DETAIL.

options.text: The column names and data types of the monthly_revenue table
options.explanation: Column names and data types are returned by DESCRIBE TABLE or DESCRIBE EXTENDED.

options.text: The schema, format, location, size, and number of files for the monthly_revenue table
options.explanation: DESCRIBE DETAIL returns table-level metadata including the format (Delta), table location (storage path), number of files, total size in bytes, partition columns, and schema name — technical details about the table itself.

questions.stem:
A data analyst creates a table using the following SQL:

```sql
CREATE TABLE analytics.customer_summary
USING DELTA
LOCATION 'abfss://container@storage.dfs.core.windows.net/customer_summary';
```

A month later, an administrator runs `DROP TABLE analytics.customer_summary`. What happens to the underlying data files?

options.text: The data files are moved to the Databricks-managed metastore storage location
options.explanation: The files are not moved — they stay at the original path.

options.text: The data files are permanently deleted from the storage account
options.explanation: Only managed tables (without LOCATION) have their data deleted on DROP TABLE.

options.text: The data files remain at the specified LOCATION and are not deleted
options.explanation: When a LOCATION clause is specified, the table is an external table. Dropping an external table removes only the table's metadata registration from the metastore, but the underlying data files at the specified path are left untouched.

options.text: The data files are archived to a separate container with a _deleted suffix
options.explanation: Databricks does not archive or rename data files when dropping an external table.

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to share a subset of sensitive columns from the `employees` table without giving direct table access. The analyst creates a view. Select TWO benefits of using a view for access control in this scenario. (Select TWO)

options.text: Users with access to the view can automatically bypass row-level security on the base table
options.explanation: Views respect the access controls and filters of the base table — they do not bypass row-level security.

options.text: The view can hide sensitive columns (such as salary or SSN) from users who only need access to non-sensitive fields
options.explanation: Views can SELECT only specific columns, effectively hiding sensitive columns from users who are only granted access to the view.

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON VIEW grants read access to the view output without exposing the underlying base table
options.explanation: Granting SELECT on a view allows users to query the view results without needing direct access to the underlying base table.

options.text: Views automatically encrypt the underlying data in the base table
options.explanation: Views are logical query definitions and have no effect on how data is physically stored or encrypted.

options.text: OPTIMIZE compacts small files into larger files; VACUUM removes files no longer referenced by the current table version but older than 168 hours
options.explanation: OPTIMIZE compacts small Parquet files into larger, optimally-sized files to improve query performance, and VACUUM physically removes data files that are no longer referenced by any table version and that are older than the specified retention period (168 hours = 7 days, which is also the default).

options.text: OPTIMIZE removes deleted records from data files; VACUUM removes transaction log entries older than 168 hours
options.explanation: OPTIMIZE does not remove deleted records — those are handled through VACUUM after they are no longer referenced.

options.text: OPTIMIZE rebuilds all indexes on the table; VACUUM removes duplicate rows
options.explanation: Delta Lake uses file-based statistics rather than traditional indexes, and VACUUM does not remove duplicate rows.

options.text: OPTIMIZE recalculates table statistics; VACUUM compacts small files into larger ones
options.explanation: OPTIMIZE is about file compaction, not statistics recalculation (ANALYZE TABLE handles statistics).

questions.stem: An analyst wants to grant a colleague read access to all tables in the `reporting` schema. Which SQL statement accomplishes this?

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON TABLE reporting.* TO `analyst@company.com`
options.explanation: The wildcard `reporting.*` is not valid SQL syntax for GRANT statements in Databricks SQL.

options.text: GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON SCHEMA reporting TO `analyst@company.com`
options.explanation: GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES gives far more than read access — it includes CREATE, MODIFY, and other privileges that are inappropriate for a read-only user.

options.text: GRANT CAN_VIEW ON SCHEMA reporting TO `analyst@company.com`
options.explanation: CAN_VIEW is a dashboard and query permission level, not a schema-level SQL privilege.

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA reporting TO `analyst@company.com`
options.explanation: GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA gives the grantee SELECT access to all current and future tables within that schema without needing to grant access to each table individually.

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to rank sales representatives by their total revenue within each region, assigning the same rank to ties and leaving no gaps in the ranking sequence. Which window function should be used?

options.text: ROW_NUMBER()
options.explanation: ROW_NUMBER() assigns unique sequential numbers even to ties, so tied reps get different ranks.

options.text: RANK()
options.explanation: RANK() assigns the same rank to ties but leaves gaps — two reps tied at rank 2 would mean the next rank is 4, not 3.

options.text: DENSE_RANK()
options.explanation: DENSE_RANK() assigns the same rank to tied values and does not leave gaps in the sequence — after two sales reps tied at rank 2, the next rank is 3, not 4.

options.text: NTILE()
options.explanation: NTILE() divides rows into a specified number of equal-sized buckets and does not rank by value.

questions.stem:
A data analyst writes the following query to find each customer's previous order date:

```sql
SELECT
  customer_id,
  order_date,
  LAG(order_date, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_order_date
FROM orders;
```

What value will `prev_order_date` contain for a customer's very first order?

options.text: NULL, because there is no previous row for the first order
options.explanation: LAG() looks back a specified number of rows within the partition. For the first row in each customer's partition there is no preceding row, so LAG returns NULL by default.

options.text: The earliest order date across all customers
options.explanation: LAG is scoped within the PARTITION BY customer_id partition and does not look across other customers' data.

options.text: The same value as order_date for that row
options.explanation: LAG does not default to the current row's value — the default for missing rows is NULL unless an explicit default is provided as the third argument.

options.text: 0, because the default offset for LAG is zero
options.explanation: 0 would only be returned if the analyst explicitly specified LAG(order_date, 1, 0) with a default of 0.

questions.stem:
A data analyst writes the following query:

```sql
WITH monthly_sales AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month,
    SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY 1
),
ranked AS (
  SELECT
    month,
    total_revenue,
    RANK() OVER (ORDER BY total_revenue DESC) AS revenue_rank
  FROM monthly_sales
)
SELECT * FROM ranked WHERE revenue_rank <= 3;
```

What does this query return?

options.text: The 3 months with the lowest total revenue
options.explanation: ORDER BY total_revenue DESC ranks highest revenue as rank 1, not the lowest.

options.text: The top 3 months by total revenue, potentially returning more than 3 rows if there are ties
options.explanation: RANK() assigns the same rank to tied values and leaves gaps. If two months are tied for rank 1, they both get rank 1, rank 2 is skipped, and the next month gets rank 3 — meaning the WHERE revenue_rank <= 3 clause could return 4 or more rows.

options.text: Exactly 3 rows showing the top 3 months by revenue with no duplicate ranks
options.explanation: RANK() (not DENSE_RANK() or ROW_NUMBER()) can return more than 3 rows when ties exist at the boundary.

options.text: An error because window functions cannot be used inside a CTE
options.explanation: Window functions are fully supported inside CTEs in Spark SQL.

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to find all customers who have NEVER placed an order. The `customers` table has a `customer_id` column, and the `orders` table also has a `customer_id` column. Which query correctly identifies customers with no orders?

options.text: SELECT c.customer_id FROM customers c LEFT ANTI JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
options.explanation: A LEFT ANTI JOIN returns only the rows from the left table (customers) that have NO matching row in the right table (orders), which is exactly the set of customers who have never placed an order.

options.text: SELECT c.customer_id FROM customers c INNER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id WHERE o.customer_id IS NULL
options.explanation: An INNER JOIN only returns matching rows — no NULL values will appear in o.customer_id after an INNER JOIN, so the WHERE clause would return no results.

options.text: SELECT c.customer_id FROM customers c FULL OUTER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id WHERE c.customer_id IS NULL
options.explanation: The WHERE c.customer_id IS NULL in a FULL OUTER JOIN would return rows where the customer exists in orders but not in the customers table (the opposite of what is needed).

options.text: SELECT c.customer_id FROM customers c RIGHT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id WHERE o.customer_id IS NULL
options.explanation: A RIGHT JOIN keeps all order rows and NULLs on the customer side for unmatched orders — not unmatched customers.

questions.stem: A data analyst needs a report showing subtotals and a grand total alongside individual rows. The table `sales(region, product, revenue)` needs to show total revenue by region, by product, and an overall grand total in one query. Select TWO SQL clauses that can produce subtotals and grand totals in a single query. (Select TWO)

options.text: GROUP BY ROLLUP(region, product)
options.explanation: ROLLUP generates subtotals for each level of the specified columns hierarchy (region+product, region only, and grand total), which covers the requirement.

options.text: GROUP BY CUBE(region, product)
options.explanation: CUBE generates subtotals for all possible combinations of the specified columns (region+product, region only, product only, and grand total), which also covers the requirement and provides more combinations.

options.text: GROUP BY region, product WITH TOTALS
options.explanation: WITH TOTALS is not valid Spark SQL syntax for GROUP BY.

options.text: GROUP BY GROUPING SETS((region, product), (region), (product), ())
options.explanation: is technically correct SQL but is not among the two canonical options — ROLLUP and CUBE are the purpose-built shorthand constructs that produce the required subtotals and grand totals more concisely.

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to calculate the number of days between a customer's first and most recent order. The `orders` table has an `order_date` column of type DATE. Which function returns the number of days between two dates?

options.text: DATE_DIFF(first_order, last_order)
options.explanation: DATE_DIFF is not a valid Spark SQL function name — the correct function is DATEDIFF.

options.text: DATE_SUB(last_order, first_order)
options.explanation: DATE_SUB(date, n) subtracts n days from a date and returns a new date, not the difference between two dates.

options.text: DATEDIFF(last_order, first_order)
options.explanation: DATEDIFF(end_date, start_date) is the Spark SQL function that returns the number of days between two DATE values, calculated as end_date minus start_date.

options.text: TIMEDIFF(last_order, first_order)
options.explanation: TIMEDIFF is not a Spark SQL function; it exists in MySQL but not in the Databricks SQL dialect.

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to extract the year and month from an order timestamp and group orders by month. The `order_ts` column is of type TIMESTAMP. Which expression correctly truncates the timestamp to the first day of the month?

options.text: DATE_TRUNC('month', order_ts)
options.explanation: DATE_TRUNC('month', order_ts) truncates a timestamp to the first day of the month at midnight (e.g., 2024-03-15 09:30:00 becomes 2024-03-01 00:00:00), which is ideal for grouping by month while retaining a proper date type.

options.text: DATE_FORMAT(order_ts, 'yyyy-MM')
options.explanation: DATE_FORMAT returns a string representation — while useful for display, it returns a string not a date, which is less appropriate for date-based grouping and calculations.

options.text: TRUNC(order_ts, 'month')
options.explanation: In Spark SQL, TRUNC() is used for numeric truncation — DATE_TRUNC is the correct function for date truncation.

options.text: EXTRACT(MONTH FROM order_ts)
options.explanation: EXTRACT(MONTH FROM ...) returns just the integer month number (1–12) and loses the year context, making it impossible to distinguish March 2023 from March 2024.

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to pivot a sales table to show each product as a column with its total revenue as the value. The table has columns `region`, `product`, and `revenue`, with products being 'Laptop', 'Tablet', and 'Phone'. Which Spark SQL syntax correctly pivots this data?

options.text: SELECT * FROM sales PIVOT (SUM(revenue) FOR product IN ('Laptop', 'Tablet', 'Phone'))
options.explanation: This is the correct Spark SQL PIVOT syntax: the PIVOT clause follows the table reference and specifies the aggregate function (SUM(revenue)), the column to pivot on (FOR product), and the explicit list of values to become columns (IN ('Laptop', 'Tablet', 'Phone')).

options.text: SELECT region, SUM(CASE WHEN product = 'Laptop' THEN revenue END) AS Laptop FROM sales GROUP BY region
options.explanation: While conditional aggregation with CASE WHEN achieves the same logical result, it is not the PIVOT syntax — it does not use the PIVOT keyword.

options.text: SELECT * FROM PIVOT(sales, product, revenue)
options.explanation: PIVOT() as a function call with positional arguments is not valid Spark SQL syntax.

options.text: SELECT region, PIVOT product ON revenue FROM sales
options.explanation: The clause order 'PIVOT product ON revenue' is not valid Spark SQL; the correct syntax uses FOR and IN.

questions.stem: A data analyst is cleaning customer data and finds that the `phone_number` column contains values like '(555) 123-4567'. The analyst needs to extract just the 10-digit number without parentheses, spaces, or dashes. Which function extracts a pattern from a string using a regular expression?

options.text: SUBSTRING(phone_number, 1, 10)
options.explanation: SUBSTRING extracts characters by position number and cannot skip non-digit formatting characters that vary in position.

options.text: SPLIT(phone_number, '-')[0]
options.explanation: SPLIT on '-' would split '123-4567' but does not handle the parentheses and space before the area code.

options.text: TRIM(REPLACE(REPLACE(REPLACE(phone_number, '(', ''), ')', ''), ' ', ''))
options.explanation: While the REPLACE chain could work, it still leaves a space between area code and the rest and is more complex and error-prone than a regex approach; more importantly, the question tests knowledge of the REGEXP_EXTRACT function.

options.text: REGEXP_EXTRACT(phone_number, '(\d{3})(\d{3})(\d{4})', 0)
options.explanation: REGEXP_EXTRACT uses a regular expression pattern to extract a specific capture group from a string — using \d{3} and \d{4} patterns matches the digit groups regardless of the surrounding formatting characters.

questions.stem: A business analyst is building a dashboard to show how total monthly revenue has changed over the past 24 months. Which chart type is most appropriate for this visualization?

options.text: Pie chart
options.explanation: Pie charts show proportional composition of a whole at a single point in time — they are not suitable for showing change over time.

options.text: Scatter plot
options.explanation: Scatter plots are designed to show the relationship (correlation) between two continuous variables, not a single metric over time.

options.text: Line chart
options.explanation: Line charts are the standard choice for showing continuous trends over time — each point represents a month's revenue and the connecting line makes temporal trends, seasonality, and direction of change visually clear.

options.text: Heatmap
options.explanation: Heatmaps show value intensity across two categorical or dimensional axes and are better suited for comparing a metric across two dimensions simultaneously.

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to display a single, prominent current-month sales total on an executive dashboard so that viewers can see the KPI at a glance. Which visualization type is most appropriate?

options.text: Counter (single value widget)
options.explanation: A Counter (also called a single value widget or KPI card) is specifically designed to prominently display a single numeric metric, making it ideal for executive KPI dashboards where the audience needs to quickly read one number.

options.text: Bar chart
options.explanation: A bar chart is appropriate for comparing values across multiple categories, not for prominently displaying a single value.

options.text: Table widget showing all monthly values
options.explanation: Showing all monthly values in a table adds noise and dilutes the impact of the single current-month figure the analyst wants to highlight.

options.text: Scatter plot
options.explanation: Scatter plots visualize correlations between two variables across many data points and are not suited for single-value KPI display.

questions.stem: A data analyst builds a Databricks SQL dashboard with a date range filter. The analyst wants clicking on a date range to automatically update three different chart widgets on the same dashboard. How should the analyst configure this?

options.text: Use a single dashboard-level date range parameter and reference the same parameter name in each chart's underlying query
options.explanation: Databricks SQL dashboards support dashboard-level parameters using the `{{ parameter_name }}` syntax — when multiple queries on the same dashboard reference the same parameter name, the dashboard filter widget controls all of them simultaneously, updating all linked charts when the parameter value changes.

options.text: Create three separate date parameters, one per chart, and configure each chart's query with its own parameter
options.explanation: Creating three separate parameters would require the user to change three filter widgets independently, which defeats the purpose of a unified filter.

options.text: Add a refresh schedule that re-runs all queries every time the dashboard is opened
options.explanation: A refresh schedule re-runs queries on a time interval — it does not respond to user-driven parameter changes.

options.text: Use a dashboard widget linking configuration to manually connect the filter to each chart after publishing
options.explanation: Databricks SQL does not have a separate 'widget linking configuration' step after publishing; parameter sharing is handled through consistent parameter naming in the queries.

questions.stem:
A data analyst creates a Databricks SQL query with the following parameter:

```sql
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE region = '{{ region }}'
```

The analyst wants users to select a region from a dropdown list populated from a `regions` lookup table. Which parameter type should be used?

options.text: Text parameter
options.explanation: A Text parameter provides a free-text input box — the user would need to manually type the exact region name, which is error-prone and not user-friendly.

options.text: Number parameter
options.explanation: A Number parameter accepts numeric input only, which is inappropriate for a string region name.

options.text: Date parameter
options.explanation: A Date parameter provides a date picker widget, which is only appropriate for date/timestamp values.

options.text: Query-based dropdown parameter
options.explanation: A Query-based dropdown parameter populates its list of selectable values by running a separate query (in this case, SELECT DISTINCT region FROM regions) and presents those values as a dropdown to the dashboard user.

questions.stem: A data analyst shares a dashboard with a team of 15 analysts. The analyst wants team members to be able to view the dashboard and run it with different parameter values, but NOT to edit the dashboard layout or change the underlying queries. Which permission level should be granted?

options.text: CAN_VIEW
options.explanation: CAN_VIEW allows users to see the cached results of the last run but does not allow them to trigger fresh query execution or change parameter values.

options.text: CAN_RUN
options.explanation: CAN_RUN permission on a Databricks SQL dashboard allows users to view the dashboard, interact with parameter widgets, trigger query execution, and see results — without being able to edit the dashboard structure or queries.

options.text: CAN_EDIT
options.explanation: CAN_EDIT would allow team members to modify the dashboard layout and queries, which the analyst wants to prevent.

options.text: CAN_MANAGE
options.explanation: CAN_MANAGE grants full administrative control including changing permissions and deleting the dashboard.

questions.stem: A data analyst is designing a Databricks SQL dashboard that will be viewed by executives every Monday morning. The dashboard queries take 45 seconds to run. Select TWO approaches that would ensure executives see current data quickly without waiting 45 seconds each time they open the dashboard. (Select TWO)

options.text: Schedule the dashboard to automatically refresh each Sunday evening so results are cached before the Monday viewing
options.explanation: Scheduling the dashboard to refresh on Sunday evening pre-populates the query result cache so that Monday morning viewers see results from the cache instantly rather than waiting for a fresh 45-second execution.

options.text: Set the query result cache TTL to 7 days to keep results available all week
options.explanation: The query result cache TTL is fixed at 24 hours in Databricks SQL and cannot be extended to 7 days — this is not a configurable option.

options.text: Pre-aggregate the data in the underlying queries to reduce query runtime
options.explanation: Pre-aggregating data (e.g., aggregating at the query level rather than returning row-level data) reduces the amount of data processed and can dramatically reduce query runtime from 45 seconds to a few seconds.

options.text: Change the dashboard to use a pie chart instead of a bar chart to reduce load time
options.explanation: The chart type (pie vs bar) has no effect on query execution time — the bottleneck is data scanning and processing, not rendering.

questions.stem: A data analyst creates an alert on a query that checks whether daily active users (DAU) drops below 1,000. The alert is configured to notify via email. The analyst runs the query manually and DAU is 850. The analyst then runs it again 30 minutes later and DAU is still 850. How many email notifications will the analyst receive across both executions?

options.text: Two notifications, one per query execution
options.explanation: Alerts do not notify on every execution where the condition is true — they notify on state changes from OK to TRIGGERED.

options.text: One notification, sent on the first execution when the condition is first met
options.explanation: Databricks SQL Alerts send a notification when the condition transitions from 'OK' to 'TRIGGERED'. On the first execution DAU is 850 (below 1,000), the alert fires and sends one email. On the second execution DAU is still 850, but the alert is already in the TRIGGERED state — it does not send another notification until the condition either recovers (goes back to OK) and then triggers again.

options.text: Zero notifications, because the alert must be triggered by the scheduled run, not a manual run
options.explanation: Alerts also evaluate when the underlying query is run manually, not only on a schedule.

options.text: Zero notifications, because the condition must be met for two consecutive periods before notifying
options.explanation: Databricks SQL Alerts do not require two consecutive periods — they trigger immediately on the first failed execution.

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to show website conversion rates by both device type (rows: Mobile, Desktop, Tablet) and day of week (columns: Mon–Sun) in a single visualization. Which chart type most clearly conveys the intensity of conversion rate across these two dimensions simultaneously?

options.text: Grouped bar chart
options.explanation: A grouped bar chart would require 7 groups of 3 bars each, making it cluttered and harder to identify patterns across both dimensions at once.

options.text: Stacked bar chart
options.explanation: A stacked bar chart shows parts of a whole rather than independent metric values across two dimensions.

options.text: Heatmap
options.explanation: A heatmap is specifically designed to display the intensity (magnitude) of a metric across two categorical dimensions simultaneously, using color to encode the value at each intersection — perfect for comparing conversion rates across device types and days of week in a compact grid.

options.text: Line chart with multiple series
options.explanation: A line chart with multiple series would show trends over time but is less effective than a heatmap for comparing a metric across two categorical dimensions without a temporal relationship.

questions.stem: A data analyst builds a dashboard with a Databricks SQL Alert set to notify when daily revenue exceeds $1,000,000. The alert query runs on a scheduled refresh every day at 6 AM. The alert is currently in the OK state (revenue was below $1M yesterday). Today at 6 AM the query runs and revenue is $1,200,000. The analyst manually changes the alert threshold to $1,500,000 at 7 AM. At 6 AM the next day, revenue is $1,300,000. Will an alert notification be sent the next morning?

options.text: Yes, because $1,300,000 exceeds the original threshold of $1,000,000
options.explanation: The original threshold of $1,000,000 was replaced by the updated threshold of $1,500,000.

options.text: Yes, because the alert was in TRIGGERED state and $1,300,000 is still above $1,000,000
options.explanation: The alert's state is re-evaluated after a threshold change — $1,300,000 against $1,500,000 resolves to OK, not TRIGGERED.

options.text: No, because $1,300,000 does not exceed the updated threshold of $1,500,000, and the condition evaluates to OK
options.explanation: After updating the threshold to $1,500,000, the alert re-evaluates against the new threshold. When the next day's revenue of $1,300,000 is checked against $1,500,000, the condition is NOT met (1,300,000 < 1,500,000), so the alert is in OK state and no notification is sent. The alert from the previous day (when $1,200,000 exceeded $1,000,000) would have already fired; the threshold change then effectively reset the evaluation context.

options.text: No, because once an alert is manually modified, it is paused until re-enabled
options.explanation: Modifying an alert threshold does not pause or disable the alert.

questions.stem: A finance team's Databricks SQL Warehouse is running large month-end reports and is billed for compute. The team is considering switching from a Classic Warehouse to a Serverless Warehouse to reduce costs. Which billing characteristic is unique to Serverless SQL Warehouses compared to Classic Warehouses?

options.text: Serverless warehouses are billed at a flat monthly rate regardless of usage
options.explanation: Serverless billing is consumption-based, not flat-rate.

options.text: Serverless warehouses are billed per second of query execution with no charge for idle startup time
options.explanation: Serverless SQL Warehouses use a per-second billing model tied to actual query execution time. Because the infrastructure is pre-provisioned by Databricks, there is minimal cold-start overhead and billing begins only when queries are actively running. Classic Warehouses bill for the entire time the underlying VMs are running (including startup, idle, and shutdown time).

options.text: Serverless warehouses are billed per query submitted, not per compute time
options.explanation: Billing is based on DBU consumption per second of compute, not a per-query flat charge.

options.text: Serverless warehouses have no billing — they are included free with the Unity Catalog subscription
options.explanation: Serverless SQL Warehouses do consume DBUs and are billed accordingly.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to prevent any single long-running query from blocking the SQL Warehouse for other users. Which warehouse configuration setting should the administrator use to automatically cancel queries that exceed a time limit?

options.text: Set the MAX_QUERY_EXECUTION_TIME property on the warehouse
options.explanation: MAX_QUERY_EXECUTION_TIME is not a Databricks SQL Warehouse configuration parameter.

options.text: Configure the STATEMENT_TIMEOUT warehouse setting to a maximum number of seconds
options.explanation: The STATEMENT_TIMEOUT setting on a SQL Warehouse defines the maximum number of seconds a query is allowed to run before it is automatically cancelled. This protects shared warehouses from runaway queries monopolizing resources.

options.text: Enable the Query Guard feature in the warehouse's advanced settings
options.explanation: 'Query Guard' is not a named Databricks SQL Warehouse feature for timeout control.

options.text: Set a QUERY_LIMIT parameter in the SQL query itself using SET syntax
options.explanation: While SET statements can configure session-level parameters, the canonical warehouse-level control for query timeouts is the STATEMENT_TIMEOUT warehouse configuration setting.

questions.stem: A data engineering team wants to query data from an external PostgreSQL database directly from Databricks SQL without first copying the data into the lakehouse. Which Databricks feature enables federated queries against external databases?

options.text: Lakehouse Federation — Databricks connects to the external database and queries it in place using a foreign catalog
options.explanation: Lakehouse Federation is the Databricks feature that allows analysts to query external databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Snowflake, Redshift, SQL Server, etc.) directly from Databricks SQL through a foreign catalog registered in Unity Catalog. Queries are pushed down to the external system where possible, and no data copy is required.

options.text: Delta Sharing — the external database administrator publishes a share that Databricks reads
options.explanation: Delta Sharing is a protocol for sharing Delta Lake data between organizations — it does not provide federated access to arbitrary relational databases.

options.text: JDBC External Table — a table defined with USING JDBC that pulls all data into a Delta table on first access
options.explanation: A JDBC External Table that pulls data on first access still materializes data into the lakehouse rather than federating the query to the source.

options.text: Unity Catalog cross-workspace sharing — the PostgreSQL workspace shares its tables with the Databricks workspace
options.explanation: Unity Catalog cross-workspace sharing is for sharing Databricks-managed data between Databricks workspaces, not for connecting to external relational databases.

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to create a fast copy of a Delta table for testing purposes without duplicating the underlying data files. The copy should share the existing data files but maintain its own independent transaction log. Which SQL command creates a shallow clone of a Delta table?

options.text: CREATE TABLE test.orders_copy CLONE prod.orders
options.explanation: `CREATE TABLE ... CLONE` is the Databricks SQL syntax for cloning a Delta table. By default it creates a shallow clone, which references the source table's data files without copying them — only the transaction log and metadata are created for the new table. This is fast and storage-efficient.

options.text: CREATE TABLE test.orders_copy AS SELECT * FROM prod.orders
options.explanation: CREATE TABLE ... AS SELECT physically copies all data into new files, consuming additional storage and taking longer than a shallow clone.

options.text: COPY INTO test.orders_copy FROM prod.orders
options.explanation: COPY INTO is used to load data from external files (e.g., CSV, JSON in cloud storage) into a Delta table, not for cloning Delta tables.

options.text: INSERT INTO test.orders_copy SELECT * FROM prod.orders
options.explanation: INSERT INTO ... SELECT also copies all data row-by-row into a pre-existing table, requiring a separate CREATE TABLE step and duplicating data files.

questions.stem: A data analyst accidentally deleted 50,000 rows from a Delta table earlier today and needs to recover them. The table has Delta Time Travel enabled and the retention period has not yet expired. Which command restores the table to its state before the deletion?

options.text: ROLLBACK TABLE sales.orders TO VERSION AS OF 5
options.explanation: ROLLBACK TABLE is not a valid Databricks SQL or Delta Lake command — Delta Lake uses RESTORE, not ROLLBACK.

options.text: RESTORE TABLE sales.orders TO VERSION AS OF 5
options.explanation: `RESTORE TABLE ... TO VERSION AS OF <n>` is the Delta Lake SQL command that restores a table to a specific historical version, recreating its state as it existed at that version. The restored state becomes the new current version (a new commit is added to the transaction log).

options.text: REVERT TABLE sales.orders USING VERSION 5
options.explanation: REVERT TABLE is not a valid Databricks SQL command.

options.text: SELECT * FROM sales.orders VERSION AS OF 5 INTO sales.orders
options.explanation: SELECT ... INTO is not valid Spark SQL syntax for inserting query results into an existing table — and reading a Time Travel version does not restore the table.

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to add a descriptive comment to a table and to a specific column so that colleagues browsing the data catalog understand what the table contains and what a specific column means. Which SQL commands accomplish this?

options.text: COMMENT ON TABLE sales.orders IS 'Daily order transactions'; COMMENT ON COLUMN sales.orders.order_status IS 'Current fulfillment status of the order'
options.explanation: `COMMENT ON TABLE ... IS ...` and `COMMENT ON COLUMN ... IS ...` are the standard Databricks SQL (and ANSI SQL) syntax for adding descriptions to tables and columns respectively. These comments are stored in the Unity Catalog metastore and appear in the data catalog UI.

options.text: ALTER TABLE sales.orders SET COMMENT 'Daily order transactions'; ALTER COLUMN sales.orders.order_status SET COMMENT 'Current fulfillment status of the order'
options.explanation: `ALTER TABLE ... SET COMMENT` and `ALTER COLUMN ... SET COMMENT` are not valid Databricks SQL syntax for table-level comments — the COMMENT ON DDL statement is the correct form.

options.text: UPDATE METADATA sales.orders SET table_comment = 'Daily order transactions', column_comment.order_status = 'Current fulfillment status'
options.explanation: UPDATE METADATA is not a valid Databricks SQL command.

options.text: DESCRIBE TABLE sales.orders COMMENT 'Daily order transactions'; DESCRIBE COLUMN sales.orders.order_status COMMENT 'Current fulfillment status'
options.explanation: DESCRIBE TABLE is a read-only command that displays metadata — it cannot be used to set comments.

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to inspect the custom properties stored on a Delta table and also compute updated column-level statistics so the query optimizer can make better plan decisions. Select TWO SQL commands that accomplish these tasks. (Select TWO)

options.text: SHOW TBLPROPERTIES sales.orders
options.explanation: `SHOW TBLPROPERTIES <table>` returns all custom key-value properties stored on a Delta table (set via ALTER TABLE ... SET TBLPROPERTIES), which is exactly how to inspect table-level metadata properties.

options.text: DESCRIBE TBLPROPERTIES sales.orders
options.explanation: DESCRIBE TBLPROPERTIES is not valid Databricks SQL syntax — the correct command is SHOW TBLPROPERTIES.

options.text: ANALYZE TABLE sales.orders COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS customer_id, order_date, revenue
options.explanation: `ANALYZE TABLE ... COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS ...` collects column-level statistics (min, max, num_nulls, distinct count, avg_col_len) for the specified columns, which the Spark query optimizer uses for better join ordering and filter cardinality estimates.

options.text: OPTIMIZE TABLE sales.orders COMPUTE STATISTICS
options.explanation: OPTIMIZE compacts files and optionally applies Z-Ordering — it does not have a COMPUTE STATISTICS clause.

questions.stem:
A data analyst writes the following query:

```sql
SELECT
  product_id,
  COALESCE(discount_pct, promo_pct, 0.0) AS effective_discount
FROM products;
```

The `discount_pct` column is NULL and `promo_pct` is 0.15 for a given row. What value does `effective_discount` return for that row?

options.text: 0.15, because COALESCE returns the first non-NULL argument
options.explanation: COALESCE evaluates its arguments left to right and returns the first value that is not NULL. In this row, discount_pct is NULL (skipped), promo_pct is 0.15 (not NULL, so it is returned). The fallback value of 0.0 is never reached.

options.text: NULL, because the first argument is NULL
options.explanation: COALESCE does not stop at NULL — it continues evaluating subsequent arguments.

options.text: 0.0, because COALESCE always returns the last argument as the fallback
options.explanation: COALESCE does not always return the last argument; it returns the FIRST non-NULL argument.

options.text: 0.0, because 0.15 is falsy and COALESCE skips falsy values
options.explanation: COALESCE checks for NULL, not for falsy/truthy values — 0.0 and 0.15 are both valid non-NULL numbers.

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to create a structured column that groups related fields together. The analyst wants to produce a column named `address` containing the sub-fields `street`, `city`, and `zip` as a struct with explicitly specified field names. Which Spark SQL function creates a named struct with custom field names?

options.text: NAMED_STRUCT('street', street, 'city', city, 'zip', zip) AS address
options.explanation: NAMED_STRUCT(name1, val1, name2, val2, ...) creates a struct with explicitly named fields. The resulting column address would have sub-fields accessible as address.street, address.city, and address.zip with exactly the names specified.

options.text: STRUCT(street, city, zip) AS address
options.explanation: STRUCT(street, city, zip) creates a struct but derives field names from the source column names automatically — it does not allow specifying custom field names, making NAMED_STRUCT the correct answer when explicit naming is required.

options.text: MAP('street', street, 'city', city, 'zip', zip) AS address
options.explanation: MAP creates a key-value map data type, not a struct — the fields are not strongly typed named sub-fields.

options.text: ARRAY(street, city, zip) AS address
options.explanation: ARRAY creates an ordered array of values of the same type, not a struct with named fields.

questions.stem: A data analyst has a table `events` with a column `payload` of type STRUCT<user_id:BIGINT, event_type:STRING, ts:TIMESTAMP>. Which expression correctly retrieves the `event_type` sub-field from the struct?

options.text: GET_FIELD(payload, 'event_type')
options.explanation: GET_FIELD is not a standard Spark SQL function for struct access.

options.text: payload['event_type']
options.explanation: The bracket notation with a string key (`payload['event_type']`) is used for MAP types, not STRUCT types — struct fields are accessed with dot notation.

options.text: payload.event_type
options.explanation: Spark SQL uses dot notation to access named fields within a struct — `payload.event_type` retrieves the event_type sub-field from the payload struct column. This is the idiomatic way to dereference struct fields in Spark SQL.

options.text: EXTRACT(event_type FROM payload)
options.explanation: EXTRACT is used for extracting components from dates and timestamps (e.g., EXTRACT(YEAR FROM ts)), not for accessing struct fields.

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to calculate a running cumulative total of revenue ordered by date. Which window function frame specification produces a true running total from the first row to the current row?

options.text: SUM(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)
options.explanation: `ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW` defines a window frame that starts at the very first row in the partition (UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) and ends at the current row — this is the canonical definition of a running total (cumulative sum).

options.text: SUM(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN CURRENT ROW AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING)
options.explanation: CURRENT ROW AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING computes a reverse-running total (from the current row to the last row), not a cumulative total from the beginning.

options.text: SUM(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date RANGE BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)
options.explanation: RANGE BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW only includes the current row and the one immediately before it (a 2-row sliding window), not all preceding rows.

options.text: SUM(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY order_date)
options.explanation: OVER (PARTITION BY order_date) without ORDER BY calculates the total per date group replicated in every row, not a running total across dates.

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to collect all product names for each customer into a single array column in the output. The source table `orders(customer_id, product_name)` has multiple rows per customer. Select TWO Spark SQL functions that aggregate multiple values into an array within a GROUP BY query. (Select TWO)

options.text: COLLECT_LIST(product_name)
options.explanation: COLLECT_LIST(col) is a Spark SQL aggregate function that collects all non-null values of the specified column into an array, including duplicates. It is the Spark-native function for this purpose.

options.text: ARRAY_AGG(product_name)
options.explanation: ARRAY_AGG(col) is the ANSI SQL standard aggregate function that collects values into an array — Databricks SQL supports ARRAY_AGG as an alias and it produces the same result as COLLECT_LIST.

options.text: STRING_AGG(product_name, ',')
options.explanation: STRING_AGG concatenates values into a delimited string, not an array data type — it returns a STRING, not an ARRAY.

options.text: GROUP_CONCAT(product_name)
options.explanation: GROUP_CONCAT is a MySQL function and is not supported in Spark SQL / Databricks SQL.

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to handle division-by-zero safely in a revenue-per-unit calculation. The analyst wants the expression to return NULL when `units_sold` is 0 rather than raising an error. Which expression most concisely achieves this in Spark SQL?

options.text: revenue / units_sold
options.explanation: In Spark SQL, dividing by zero for integer types raises a runtime error (for floating-point it returns Infinity) — no null-safe behavior without an explicit guard.

options.text: CASE WHEN units_sold = 0 THEN NULL ELSE revenue / units_sold END
options.explanation: The CASE expression achieves the same result but is more verbose than NULLIF — the question asks for the most concise approach.

options.text: revenue / NULLIF(units_sold, 0)
options.explanation: NULLIF(units_sold, 0) returns NULL when units_sold equals 0, making the division NULL/NULL safely without error. This is the most concise and idiomatic way to guard against division-by-zero in Spark SQL.

options.text: TRY_DIVIDE(revenue, units_sold)
options.explanation: TRY_DIVIDE is not a standard Spark SQL function — the correct approaches are NULLIF or a CASE expression.

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to embed a published Databricks SQL dashboard inside an internal company portal web page so employees can view the dashboard without logging into Databricks. Which mechanism allows embedding a Databricks SQL dashboard in an external web page?

options.text: Use an HTML iframe pointing to the dashboard's public embed URL, using Databricks' embedded dashboard feature
options.explanation: Databricks SQL supports embedding dashboards in external web applications using an iframe with the dashboard's embed URL. This allows organizations to surface live dashboard views within internal portals, wikis, or other web tools without requiring users to navigate to the Databricks workspace directly.

options.text: Export the dashboard as a static PDF and link to the file in the portal
options.explanation: Exporting as a static PDF creates a snapshot — it is not a live interactive embedding.

options.text: Grant the portal server CAN_MANAGE permission on the dashboard so it can fetch and render it
options.explanation: CAN_MANAGE is an administrative permission level, not an embedding mechanism, and granting it to a portal server would be a security risk.

options.text: Databricks SQL dashboards cannot be embedded in external applications — they must be viewed within the Databricks workspace
options.explanation: Embedded dashboard iframes are a supported Databricks SQL feature.

questions.stem: A data analyst is building a Databricks SQL dashboard with a multi-value dropdown parameter so that users can select one or more regions and the query filters to all selected regions. Select TWO TRUE statements about implementing multi-value parameters in Databricks SQL. (Select TWO)

options.text: To use multiple selected values in an IN clause, the query should use the syntax WHERE region IN ({{ region }}), where the parameter is configured as multi-value
options.explanation: When a parameter is configured as multi-value, Databricks SQL handles the formatting so that `{{ region }}` inside an IN clause is expanded correctly to match multiple selected values — for example, WHERE region IN ({{ region }}) becomes WHERE region IN ('East', 'West', 'North') when those three regions are selected.

options.text: Multi-value parameters are configured by setting the parameter type to 'Query Dropdown' and enabling the 'Allow multiple values' option
options.explanation: The multi-value capability is enabled through the 'Allow multiple values' setting on a Query Dropdown parameter type, which allows users to select more than one value from the dropdown list.

options.text: A multi-value parameter stores the selected values as a comma-separated string that can be used with the IN clause by using the `{{ parameter_name }}` syntax directly without any IN() wrapping
options.explanation: Using the parameter outside an IN() wrapper does not properly expand multiple values for filtering — the parameter must be placed inside IN({{ region }}) to work correctly with multiple selections.

options.text: Multi-value parameters are not supported in Databricks SQL; analysts must use a separate query per region
options.explanation: Multi-value parameters are a supported feature in Databricks SQL dashboards.
